scenarios.title: Tanker leak at I-95 mile 22
scenarios.outcomeType: Decision sequence — isolate, communicate, request resources

welcome.title: Scenario overview
welcome.body: You are an Engine 4 firefighter responding to a single-vehicle incident on I-95. Today we focus on isolation discipline. 70% of preventable exposures happen in the first 90 seconds because the perimeter wasn't moved when the wind shifted.
welcome.reassurance: We aren't testing you; we're rehearsing the muscle memory you need when minutes matter. There is no penalty for taking your time inside the scenario.
welcome.expectedOutcome: Demonstrate isolation, communication, and clean handoff under pressure.

scenarios.context: Light mist drifting across the southbound lanes. A bystander says she smelled fuel. You see UN 1203 placards. Wind is from the SW at ~10 mph. Two vehicles are stopped within 30m of the tanker.

steps.title: First 60 seconds
steps.coachHint: Start with what protects everyone — including you.

options.label: Approach the tanker to read the BOL
options.feedback: You closed the gap before establishing isolation — this is how responders become patients.
options.insight: You closed the gap to gather information before establishing safety; in the next round, lead with the perimeter.

options.label: Establish 50m isolation upwind, hold position
options.feedback: Good. You bought yourself a thinking margin and protected the bystanders downwind.

options.label: Begin moving stopped vehicles
options.feedback: Reasonable instinct, but isolation comes first. Moving people while exposed is a coin flip.
options.insight: You moved to protect bystanders, but exposed yourself before setting the perimeter.

steps.title: Sitrep to dispatch
steps.prompt: Compose the sitrep. Lead with the picture, not the timeline.
steps.voicePrompt: Engine 4 on scene I-95 southbound mile 22, single tanker UN 1203, active leak with vapor, wind south-west ten, fifty meter isolation set, requesting hazmat one and PD for traffic.
steps.coachHint: Be specific. Location, product, hazard, wind, isolation, resources.
steps.modelAnswer: Engine 4 on scene I-95 SB mile 22, single tanker UN 1203, active leak with vapor, wind SW 10, 50m isolation set, requesting hazmat 1 and PD for traffic.

steps.title: Driver in the isolation zone
steps.prompt: Hazmat 1 is 9 minutes out. A driver in the isolation zone steps out and walks toward you.
steps.coachHint: Move people, don't move the perimeter unnecessarily.

options.label: Wave him toward you, away from the tanker
options.feedback: Right call. Move him crosswind, not toward the source, and document exposure time.
options.insight: You moved a civilian out of risk without expanding your exposure.

options.label: Tell him over PA to stay in his vehicle
options.feedback: Defensible — but a stationary vehicle in a vapor plume isn't a safe shelter.
options.insight: You chose stand-off over intervention; the call was defensible but left a civilian inside the plume.

options.label: Send your partner in to retrieve him
options.feedback: You just doubled exposure without PPE. Hazmat 1 will arrive to two patients.
options.insight: You committed personnel into the hazard before specialty resources arrived.

steps.title: Briefing the arriving IC
steps.coachHint: Picture before chronology. The new IC needs the situation, not the story.

options.label: Location, product, isolation, resources, life safety
options.feedback: Clean handoff. The new IC starts thinking, not catching up.
options.insight: You led with the picture, not the chronology — the receiving IC could plan instead of catch up.

options.label: What you've already done in chronological order
options.feedback: Useful, but slower. Lead with the picture, then the actions.
options.insight: Your handoff was complete but timeline-led; the picture-first version travels faster under pressure.

options.label: Your uncertainty about the wind direction
options.feedback: Lead with what you know. Save uncertainties for the second beat.
options.insight: You opened with uncertainty; that frames the new IC's first decision around doubt instead of action.

scenarios.title: 78F, altered, fever — triage decision

welcome.title: Scenario overview
welcome.body: You are an RN in the Emergency Department. Today we focus on early sepsis recognition. One in three sepsis-related delays trace back to the first 60 seconds of triage.
welcome.reassurance: We aren't testing you; we're building your muscle memory. Approach this as a safe space to practice the qSOFA frame.
welcome.expectedOutcome: Recognize, escalate, and start the hour-1 bundle without losing time on the wrong protocol.

scenarios.context: 78-year-old female brought in by family for confusion since this morning. Vitals: T 38.6, HR 112, RR 24, BP 98/56, SpO2 95% RA. Family says she's been on antibiotics for a UTI for 2 days.

steps.title: Initial impression
steps.prompt: Initial triage. What do you flag?
steps.coachHint: Hold the qSOFA frame: respiratory rate, mentation, blood pressure.

options.label: qSOFA positive — escalate now
options.feedback: RR 24, altered, SBP <100 — three of three. Don't wait on cultures to escalate.
options.insight: You recognized the qSOFA pattern early and escalated without waiting on confirmation.

options.label: Possible UTI worsening, route to fast-track
options.feedback: She meets qSOFA criteria. Fast-track delays the hour-1 bundle.
options.insight: You anchored on the simpler diagnosis; the qSOFA frame was sitting in front of you.

options.label: Hold and reassess in 15 minutes
options.feedback: Watchful waiting here costs you the bundle window.
options.insight: You chose watchful waiting in a window that rewards velocity.

steps.title: What do you order, and in what order?
steps.prompt: Compose your first orders. Velocity beats perfect ordering, but cultures should precede antibiotics where feasible.
steps.voicePrompt: Lactate, blood cultures times two before antibiotics, broad spectrum antibiotics, thirty mils per kilo crystalloid, prep norepinephrine if MAP stays under sixty-five after fluids.
steps.coachHint: Be specific. What you order, what comes first, what triggers escalation.
steps.modelAnswer: Lactate, blood cultures x2, broad-spectrum antibiotics, 30 mL/kg crystalloid, and prep norepinephrine if MAP stays <65 after fluids.

steps.title: After 1L crystalloid
steps.coachHint: Finish the bundle's fluid target before escalating to pressors unless she's not tolerating fluids.

options.label: Continue fluids to 30 mL/kg total, reassess MAP
options.feedback: Right path. You haven't completed the bundle's fluid target yet.
options.insight: You stayed inside the bundle protocol rather than escalating off-script.

options.label: Start norepinephrine immediately
options.feedback: Defensible if she's not tolerating fluids — but MAP 66 buys you time to finish the resuscitation.
options.insight: You escalated to pressors before completing the fluid target; defensible, but bundle-divergent.

options.label: Hold fluids, recheck in 30 minutes
options.feedback: You'd be undertreating an actively septic patient.
options.insight: You held therapy in a window that requires forward motion.
